items.q: Is a golf putt an open or closed skill?
items.correct: Closed - it is self-paced in a stable environment.
items.misconception: Open, because golf is played outdoors.
items.random_wrong: It is a gross skill, so it must be open.
items.why: Classification depends on how predictable the environment is, not on the sport or muscle group.

items.q: When is part practice better than whole practice?
items.correct: For complex or dangerous skills that can be broken into sections.
items.misconception: For every skill, because more breaks are always better.
items.random_wrong: Only for skills that cannot be divided.
items.why: Breaking down a skill helps with complex or risky actions, but simple skills are best practised whole.

items.q: Why do beginners rely on extrinsic feedback?
items.correct: They cannot yet judge their own performance using internal cues.
items.misconception: Beginners should only use intrinsic feedback.
items.why: Novices lack the experience to feel their errors, so external guidance is essential at first.

items.q: A golf putt is best classified as a:
items.why: Correct answer: Closed skill. “Open skill” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Part practice is most useful for:
items.why: Correct answer: Complex or dangerous skills. “Very simple skills” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Feedback from a coach is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Extrinsic feedback. “Intrinsic feedback” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An open skill requires the performer to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Adapt to a changing environment. “Repeat the same fixed movement” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
